speaker: interviewer
text: The date is May 7th, 1996. The survivor is Boris Chartan. The interview is taking place in Plainview, New York, the United States of America. It's taking place in English. And I'm interviewing him. And my name is Anne Bernard. Let's talk about in 1937, your first inkling of war occurred when some people were coming through from Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in 1937, Czechoslovakian Jews that ran away from Hitler were coming across to our-- the whole area there. And, of course, in the small town, where would they come first? They came to shul, to see Jewish families, and to see where they can be taken, and what. And they would tell their stories and the atrocities. And I would listen in as a young kid. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] even the older people did not want to believe the stories that I head.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was just like they tried to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not hear them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When I look back now, how could that had happened? Nobody wanted to hear what they were telling us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's why I'm so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bent on, and the Holocaust, has to be talked about, repeated, really, because some of the Jews in those days couldn't believe that something terrible like this could happen. And these people came. And they were barefoot in the coldest day, I saw them. With clothes that was all ripped already, because they were probably wandering for months and months until they reached this town. And, of course, our house was a very, very hospitable house. My mother never knew who would come home Friday night for supper. If my father would go to shul, and there was a stranger or somebody, we would always have strange people for supper in our house. And these people came to our house. And I recall as a child sitting around, you know, they're telling their stories, and their plight. This is the first time that I became aware of what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that the adults there understood 100%, or in any way were compassionate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were compassionate. But, like, you couldn't believe-- I don't know whether they couldn't believe, or they just couldn't handle it at that time. I mean, we knew there was anti-Semitism. And we didn't live in a paradise in Podkamien. But you didn't think that it was this kind of a thing happening not too far from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they fearful at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think, really, that they realized to be fearful. They couldn't believe. Yes, we knew there was anti-Semitism. We're going to be robbed. We're going to be beaten. We're going to be this. But they could not believe that something like this is really taking place, where you-- your store is closed up. You're just chased off completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your reactions were at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started a lot of questioning. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] still going to [NON-ENGLISH], even then, I recall once that a group of us were sitting with this Rabbi Akiva. And we started questioning all these things to him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We challenged it, and we asked the question, if there's a God, how could this happen, what we're hearing? And the only answer really-- and he had no answer, which I could realize. But he said to us, you must not question God.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, at that age, being impressed with your rabbi, it kind of really made an impression on me. I'm not faulting him for it. It's just that he had no answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you notice anything that was increased in the public schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, anti-Semitism started to increase quickly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The teachers became harsh on us. We were penalized for not coming on the holidays to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, our marks were starting to-- they just did whatever they wanted with our marks. It didn't matter how good you were. I knew I always did my work. I was very good in math. That was my strongest point in school, always. And I could just see it, what they were doing to me. I mean, when I look back, they were just-- no matter what I did, I was always wrong. Geometry I was good at. And it just didn't suit them. And they could that what they will. It's not like you could go to the Board of Education and complain, and that they ran the school, period. They had the final say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you complain to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And, really, just coming home complaining was all right. But even then, I realized there was very little they could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you cope with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just kept plugging, and sticking with it. My dad always says, just stick with it. And that's the best we can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that in any way frustrate you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It frustrated me to a point, where maybe at times [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt that my hard work and everything is to null, because you aim for marks. And-- but I realized that the alternative would be, you can't get out of school. It was compulsory. So you might as well stick with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember ever feeling any fear in school, as time went on?

speaker: interviewee
text: As time went on, the only fear that we had is getting beaten up. But it became a part of life, that you just accepted it, going home from school, and every once in a while, getting punched out. And there was no way of fighting back, because you were outnumbered. So you took your lickings, and you went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember feeling-- did you cry a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I am not one that cries easy. I hurt inside a lot. But I do not cry a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did all of the Jewish children going to the school have the same experiences as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [? Remember ?] a few of them that are-- there's only two that are left from my time that were in my class. And sometimes, we just rehash the thing on the phone. I don't seem them too often. One lives in Florida. One lives in Florida. But when we talk, we just bring this issue up sometimes. And they had the same feelings as I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in the gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in the gymnasium till 1941. Up to 1939, this was a Polish school. In 1939, when the war broke out, the Russians came into the part of our town-- to our town. And they took over part of Poland. There was a dividing line. And then the school became a Russian run school, which-- there was maybe less polish anti-Semitism, because the Russians had brought in a lot of their teachers. But, nonetheless, they opened up the school on six days a week. And being an observant Jew, I never went to school on Saturday. And what I recall is this Russian lady teacher would walk right in on Shabbat into shul looking for me, and insisting that I go to school. But, of course, I didn't budge. And that-- that, also, when I look back, bred a lot of anti-Semitism on their part. They were trying to force us to do something that we never did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two years that-- then we had to start learning Russian too. So the last two years, I had to take up the Russian language too, besides the Polish language. It was just learning another language. That wasn't too much. And-- Did you find it an easy language to learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it wasn't too hard, because it almost-- if you can speak Polish, you can understand Russian when somebody speaks. So it's just a question of pronouncing it. And the writing is a little different, but not that much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you leave at the age of 15?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the age of 15, I left because that's when-- in September, Rosh Hashanah [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I was just trying to-- the Russians came in. In July of 1941, the Nazis came in. And then there was no more school for the Jewish kids. So it wasn't that I left school. It was just that we were not allowed to go to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just studied [NON-ENGLISH] unofficially, and just continued just to study Hebrew and Yiddish, and no more of the other, because the [NON-ENGLISH] itself was not geared for math and all the other stuff. They were not geared to teachers. I did have a private tutor that used to come to the house, just to continue the study with me. He had graduated college in [NON-ENGLISH], and came back. He was born, and his family lived in, Podkamien. And his name was Kesler, K-E-S-L-E-R. His name was [NON-ENGLISH] Kesler. And he's the one that still taught me for about a year. He used to give me private tutor lessons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the Nazis walking around the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they came in sometimes in July. And at that time, I think we realized more what to expect, because you heard already what happened to the other half part of Poland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We all hid in a cellar. And we heard the noise of the shooting, when they were advancing, and the Russians were running away. But that's when they chased the Russians out of there. And this went on all night long. And we could hear the noise. And they were very noisy, the Russian-- the Germany army I mean. When they came in, they made a lot of noise, just like frightening. We were all afraid. I recall that morning, my father just walked out the front of the door to see what had happened overnight. And the German soldier grabbed him, grabbed him, and grabbed a number of lot of Jews-- I'd say maybe 25 or 30 Jews. And they brought them out in the middle of the town, which was like a marketplace. And the marketplace was in the front of our house. And they made them lay down. And they put a soldier with a machine gun. And we were very frightened. And we-- I, personally, did not see it. But I knew when my dad came back. And we had a gentile man who interceded on behalf. He was like the-- I'd say the mayor of the town. He was a fine gentleman. And somehow or other, by-- it would have been about 7:00 o'clock in the morning that they all got picked up. And about 3:00 o'clock in the afternoon, my dad returned home, which was very lucky, because some of the cases they would shoot them. And somehow or other, they were not shot at that time. They let them go, because of this righteous gentile that interceded on behalf of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when he came home? Did you come to any decisions?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he came, we knew what the situation is. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was very little-- there was no place to run after that. Everybody just stayed put in town. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had calmed down. The work of the German army probably was not right away to kill people. They were more interested in, you know, advancing the front to move ahead. Some of them stood in between our houses, I recall there. A few of them [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we're not harassing us. The only thing I recall is one of them saying to my mother, a soldier, that he's sorry because if his mother how he was behaving, it would be terrible, towards Jews. I recall that, my mother telling him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family do anything differently because the Nazis were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was nothing to do. The stores were closed up. There was no more stores. You just lived them on what you had. And the front moved on. And then the Gestapo came in, which was the next. And they merged with the Ukrainians, as they became the police in town. There was no more Polish police. It was Ukrainian police, under the direction of the Gestapo. And they started to harass us, and pick out individuals. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What is outstanding is they came in on a Monday. By Friday already that same week, the shul was burned. They did all their dirty work on Friday. We knew this from stories before, that most of the terrible things will take on Friday. So I recall just looking out the window of my house, and watching the shul burn. And they grabbed Jews, and cut their beards, and threw them in to cold water. I mean, some terrible things that were happening that Friday. And the shul burned from Friday till about Sunday, till it completely burned down. There was nothing left, just ashes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the three other shuls in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The three other shuls, they just broke the windows, and just desecrated the whole thing. They did not burn them. They just burned the main shul, which was a beautiful shul. It was a piece of art, I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: You think that's why they burnt it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, because it was a very artistic shul. It's-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How does--

speaker: interviewee
text: It had a dome on it. It had-- sorry. And it had a lot of artwork. There was artists brought in to paint the eastern wall. It was just magnificent. And the candelabras, that shul was lit just with candles. And hundreds and hundreds of candles were burned down Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the reaction of the people in the town, and your family's reaction to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was frightening. We knew already that this is it. Not that we didn't realize whether we were going to go to concentration camps, or to ghettos. But we knew that life was going to get much worse for Jews. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They made us do some work. Podkamien means the city under the rocks, because there was a big mountain. And there was two big rocks that were growing out of the mountain. They made us march, go to work, to blow up these mountains so they can get the rocks out of it. So I as a young kid, already-- then I was-- '41, I was 15 years old. I was working alongside men, and doing that kind of work, blowing up mountains. In the winter time, they'd take us. And we'd just clear the roads from snow, again, because winter sets in early in November. And there's a lot of snow in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the purpose of you dynamiting the rocks?

speaker: interviewee
text: So that they used the rocks to build roads. They were building roads. They wanted good roads so that they can move their machinery up the front lines, as they were marching towards the Russian border. We were probably [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 100 kilometers from the Russian border, which wasn't too far.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family was intact at this time. You still lived in the same house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Still lived in the same house, just me, my mom, and my dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had died. My grandparents had died earlier, the Chartan grandparents. My grandfather died in '36. And my grandmother died about-- either late '37 or early '38. She was mostly an [INAUDIBLE] most of her years that I can remember, very bad, arthritic-ridden. She was almost bed-ridden for the longest time that I could remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your cousins who also shared this house with you? Were they still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were all still there. Everybody was still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many of those people worked dynamiting the mountain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost every one of them, except one of them-- [NON-ENGLISH] Stein was taken away by the Russians. And we don't know what happened to him. He never came back. He was gone before the Germans came in. The Russians took a group of people with them when they left. Some went voluntary. And some, they took in the army, like, and I never heard from him anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have enough to eat during this period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were lucky because my dad had a lot of friends, gentiles. And my grandfather living in the village seven kilometers away, and he was a pretty well to do individual. So food for us at that period was not a problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're feeling of-- how did you feel that you were suffering at this time? Could you describe that to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you were afraid to go out. You were beaten up when you're taken to work. You were not allowed to go out in the street at certain times. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Every once in a while, the Ukrainian police would pick to kill a Jew. I'd watch somebody being shot, as a kid, looking, and maybe 500 feet away from me, I saw men walk out [INAUDIBLE] his name was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was-- had the same type of leather good store as my dad. And I saw him being marched out of the back of his house. Two seconds later, he was shot in back of the head. And he just fell over. And this would go on, from time to time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think the reasons are that your family stayed intact?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very close. And I think my dad was the one that guided us, and watched over us, and knew how to-- every time, when there was some problems, how to deal with it to keep us together. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The first time that they came in, and they grabbed a bunch of people to be sent to Sasow, the camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was grabbed as a young boy in the street. And as the transport was leaving, my dad exchanged himself for me. He went to Sasow. And I remained with my mother. So that's the time that we were separated. And [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] the Polish man that was a very close friend of my dad's, Mr. [POLISH], came up and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] said to us, if we ever needed, we could come to his house and stay with him. In the meantime, he used to go about once a month to Sasow, which I would say was 25 kilometers or so, and bring some packages of food to my dad to camp there. That's how close he was to my dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it that your father was taken to Sasow?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year that my father was taken to Sasow was in the year of 1942, Sasow in the year of 1942, in the fall.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were approximately what age then?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did your father stay in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father stayed in that camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] up to the-- sometimes in the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] summer of the following year, which would bring us up to 1943. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In between, I was taken to Sasow. And from Sasow, they had sent me to Lackie, which was about 20 kilometers away or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you taken to Sasow?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- grabbed again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you grabbed?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Podkamien, we still stayed in our house. And every once in awhile, they'd have to give a quote of people. And it was my quota that I had to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still dynamiting the mountains at this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were still working there. But that had nothing to do with it. They wanted [? a ?] [? quarter ?] up to Sasow to go to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your feelings when you were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, frightened. I brought there, my dad was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. But the sad part was when I-- I figured, well, at least I was with him. But then when they took me to Lackie they sent me from there. We were separated again. And I ran away from Lackie a month later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think they sent your to Lackie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they needed some people. It wasn't for any special reason. They just picked on a group of people. And they sent us over there to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing, mining for rocks, building roads. They kept us on the road building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were those camps near a railroad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sasow did not have a railroad. The nearest railroad was in [POLISH].
